topic: Frozen But Alive: The Molecular Survival Tricks of Arctic Animals
title: How Antifreeze Proteins Let Fish, Frogs, and Insects Survive Deadly Ice

lesson.learn:
Some animals can survive freezing solid because evolution gave them biochemical tools for coping with ice. Instead of letting cells rupture or dry out fatally, these animals use cryoprotectants, antifreeze proteins, controlled water management, and precise timing to survive temperatures that would kill most mammals. Wood frogs, certain insects, and some fish are among the best-known examples.
The feat is astonishing, but it is not magic. Survival depends on limiting damage during freezing and then restarting metabolism safely when conditions improve.
lesson.simpler: Some animals survive freezing because their bodies make special chemicals that protect cells from ice damage.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Freezing survival is a chemistry problem solved by evolution: the animal has to control ice, water, and cell damage all at once.
lesson.deeperDive:
Different species use different strategies. Wood frogs can tolerate large portions of body water turning to ice by flooding tissues with glucose and other cryoprotectants. Certain fish and insects produce antifreeze proteins that interfere with harmful crystal growth. Some animals supercool, meaning they avoid freezing altogether even below 0 C for a time. Others survive by controlling where ice forms and how fast it spreads.
These systems are a reminder that the boundaries of life are narrower than we imagine, but still very real. Freeze tolerance has limits, and it usually comes with tradeoffs in activity, habitat, and metabolism. The science is useful far beyond curiosity, too, because cryobiology and organ preservation both borrow ideas from these natural solutions.
lesson.funFact: A wood frog can look dead while frozen and then return to activity after thawing.
references: Wikimedia Commons image metadata for amphibian and insect imagery.

people: Kenneth Storey - Researcher known for freeze-tolerance and cryoprotectant studies.
Janet Storey - Researcher in animal hibernation and cryobiology.
Cryobiologists - Scientists who study how life survives cold.
Wildlife biologists - They document freeze-tolerant species in nature.
places: Boreal forests - Home to some freeze-tolerant species.
Wetlands - Places where wood frogs and other amphibians overwinter.
Arctic and subarctic regions - Harsh environments that favor cold adaptation.
Laboratories - Where freezing and thawing are studied carefully.
years: 1990s - Cryoprotectants and antifreeze proteins are studied in depth.
2000s - Biomedicine begins borrowing from animal cold-survival strategies.
concepts: Cryoprotectant - A substance that protects cells during freezing.
Antifreeze protein - A molecule that interferes with ice crystal growth.
Supercooling - Staying unfrozen below the normal freezing point.
Freeze tolerance - The ability to survive internal ice formation.
Cryobiology - The study of life in cold conditions.
